Exploring Suicidal and Homicidal Ideation 475 
region where the most subtle nuances of facial expression or hesi¬ 
tancy of speech may indicate that suicide is imminent. 
This chronological approach is very simple. That is exactly why 
it works. It is easy to remember. In fact, it is actually hard to forget. 
Most clinicians tell me that once they have studied it, it naturally 
pops into their minds as soon as they begin an exploration of 
suicidal ideation. All that remains is to examine what information 
is important to explore in each time frame, always attempting to 
simplify the task by organizing the material into logical miniregions 
and strategies. We will now examine each chronological region in 
detail, in the same order we would explore them in an actual 
interview. 
Step 1: Exploration of Presenting Suicidal Events 
If a patient presents with a suicide attempt or gesture, it becomes 
critical to understand the severity of the event, because a serious 
suicide attempt represents one of the criteria of our Triad of Lethality 
and may well indicate the need for prompt hospitalization. But what 
information are we seeking? What would tell us that this was a 
dangerous event and that hospitalization might be necessary? The 
answer seems to lie in asking a series of questions that give the 
clinician an idea of how close the patient came to killing himself or 
herself and how does the patient feel about the fact that he or she 
did not die. The following information can help provide answers to 
these two pressing questions: 
1. How did the patient try to kill himself? (e.g.. What method 
was used?) 
2. How serious was the action taken with this method? (e.g.. If the 
patient overdosed, what pills and how many were taken? If the 
patient cut himself, where did he cut and did the cut require 
stitches?) 
3. To what degree did the patient intend to die? (e.g.. Did the 
patient tell anyone about the attempt afterwards? Did the patient 
hint to anyone beforehand? Did the patient do the attempt in 
an isolated area or in a place where he or she was likely to be 
found? Did the patient write a will or suicide note, check on 
insurance, or say good-bye to significant others in the days 
preceding the event?) 
4. How does the patient feel about the fact that he or she did not 
successfully kill himself or herself? (A very good question here 
is to simply ask, “What are some of your thoughts about the 
fact that you are still alive now?") 
5. How well planned was this attempt as opposed to an impul¬ 
sive act? 

============================================================
PAGE 504 of 792
============================================================
476 Advanced Techniques of Interviewing 
6. Did alcohol or drugs play a role in the attempt? 
7. Were interpersonal factors a major role in the attempt, including 
feelings of failure, “people would be better off without me/ 
and anger toward people (a suicide attempt undertaken to make 
others feel guilt)? 
8. Was there a specific stressor or set of stressors that prompted 
the attempt? 
9. At the time of the attempt, how hopeless did the patient feel? 
10. Why did the attempt fail? (e.g.. How was the patient found, and 
how did the patient end up getting help?) 
As one can see, answers to these questions can provide a powerful 
window into the internal world of the patient at the time of the 
attempt. Such a window provides the best information available 
with regard to how serious the patient was during this attempt. All 
the statistical risk factors in the world will not tell us if a specific 
patient intended death. Excluding patients who may accidentally 
kill themselves during manipulative gestures, people kill themselves 
because they have decided to do so. It is not only an act of the 
heart, but it is also a cognitive decision of the mind. The above 
information gives us direct access into the shadowy recesses of this 
decision-making process. 
Obviously, it is important to uncover the information described 
above, but at first glance this appears to be an intimidating list of 
things to remember. To some degree it is, if one tries to memorize 
it. We need a strategy. Fortunately, we already have one. In the first 
chapter of this book, we discussed the power of a validity technique, 
designed by Pascal, called the “Behavioral Incident." If you recall, 
the clinician uses this technique every time that he or she does one 
of two things: asks for a specific piece of data (e.g., "Did you put 
the gun up to your head?") or asks the patient to continue a descrip¬ 
tion of what happened sequentially (e.g., “Tell me what you did 
next"). Using the behavioral incident technique, there is a way to 
gather almost all of the above data without even having to think 
about the separate bits for the most part. Simply ask the patient to 
"walk you through" the suicide incident itself, from front to back. 
In essence ask the patient to create a "verbal videotape" step by 
step. If the patient does this, then almost all of the material described 
above will surface naturally. 
Let us see this strategy at work with a 52-year-old successful 
businessman, who we will call Mr. Leland. Last night he was seen 
in the emergency room by a member of the crisis team, following 
an overdose. He refused hospitalization but agreed to see a therapist 
the next day for possible inclusion in a crisis support group. Much 

============================================================
PAGE 505 of 792
============================================================
Exploring Suicidal and Homicidal Ideation 477 
of his pain arises from the unexpected loss of his wife of 30 years, 
who died of a ruptured aneurysm about 3 months ago. 
Mr. Leland is a man who was greatly subdued by his pain. He 
speaks surprisingly quietly, and with a weary hesitation that one 
would not necessarily expect from a high-powered executive. He is 
nicely groomed, and as one would expect from an executive, he is 
all too well tooled in the fine art of putting up a pleasant front 
during difficult times. His short black hair, like his voice, is quietly 
receding. Occasionally he manages a genuine but weary smile, the 
look of a strong man resigned to his own weakening. Let us pick 
up the conversation as the clinician begins an exploration of the 
presenting suicide event: 
Clin.: It's my understanding from the crisis worker, 
Elizabeth, that . . . 
Pt.: By the way, a very nice person. 
Clin.: Yes, she is. And very good too. In any case Elizabeth 
told me you had overdosed last night. Tell me a little 
bit about what happened. 
Pt.: Well, not much to say. I just felt like I couldn't take it 
any more, and I took a bunch of pills. 
Clin.: Why don't you sort of walk me through what 
happened last night, so I get a better feel for what 
happened. For instance when did you start to think 
about killing yourself yesterday? 
Pt.: That's hard to say. I've been thinking about it a lot . . . 
(pause). I find weekends really hard. I miss her so 
much. I do better during the work week when I'm 
busy. The weekends seem endless. Anyway, after 
dinner I just sort of felt overwhelmed. I was listening 
to a song we loved, and I just couldn't stop crying. 
Clin.: It sounds very very painful, well beyond words 
(patient nods his head in agreement). What happened 
next? 
Pt.: Well I just took the pills. 
Clin.: Now where did you keep the pills? 
Pt.: In the medicine cabinet. 
Clin.: Had you stockpiled any pills, you know, with the 
intention of maybe killing yourself? 
Pt.: Yea, I'm ashamed to say so, but I had. 

============================================================
PAGE 506 of 792
============================================================
478 Advanced Techniques of Interviewing 
Clin.: What makes you say you are ashamed to say so? 
Pt.: It just seems weak, an easy way out, it's not me. 
Clin.: It doesn't sound weak to me, with all the pain you are 
feeling, it's pretty common to have thoughts of killing 
oneself. You know I see this less as a sign of weakness 
and more as a sign of how much you loved your wife. 
Pt.: (tilts his head and squints his eyes) Hmm. Yea, I guess 
that's possible. 
Clin.: But back to the overdose itself, how many bottles had 
you stored up? 
Pt.: Fifteen, twenty. 
Clin.: So you'd been thinking about this awhile? 
Pt.: Oh yea. 
Clin.: Had you made out your will or done other things like 
written suicide notes? 
Pt.: As a matter of fact, I had written a note for my son, 
Bernie. My will, it was already in order. 
Clin.: Now when you took the pills what pills did you take? 
Pt.: Some Tylenol, some Benadryl, and some type of 
sleeping med, I forget what it was. 
Clin.: How many pills did you take? 
Pt.: Not a lot. 
Clin.: Roughly how many, 30, 40? 
Pt.: No not that much, maybe 15, 20 max. 
Clin.: What stopped you? 
Pt.: I'm not really sure . . . (pause). I think part of it was 
my son, I tore the letter up when I was done. I don't 
think it's fair to him, to have to deal with another 
death, not after what he's been through. I think I'm 
just going to have to deal with this (hand goes up to 
his brow and he begins to sob) but I'm not sure I can. 
Clin.: Well I'm glad you came in. Obviously you're in a lot 
of pain, and you're obviously a very strong man. 
Maybe we can help you to deal with this better. 
Pt.: I hope so ... . God, I hope so. 

============================================================
PAGE 507 of 792
============================================================
Exploring Suicidal and Homicidal Ideation 479 
Clin.: I know you've been in a tremendous amount of pain 
recently. Sometimes that makes people feel hopeless. 
Were you feeling hopeless last night? 
Pt.: Pretty hopeless, but I feel a little better today, I think I 
need to go on, killing myself isn't the answer. 
Clin.: What makes you say that? 
Pt.: I stopped didn't I? 
Clin.: Yes, I guess you did. I'm struck by your strength and 
your love for your son too. You know, when you say 
you stopped, what did you do after you stopped? 
Pt.: Well I called my best friend, Peter. I told him what 
was going on. He wasn't surprised. He told me he'd 
been worried about me and thought we needed to go 
to the E.R. I was so damn- exhausted I just did what he 
said and off we went. 
Clin.: How do you feel about the fact you're still alive? 
Pt.: (long pause) I don't know (pause). I guess it's O.K. I 
need some help of some sort, if I'm going to go on. I 
can't go on like this indefinitely. 
Clin.: I think we can get you some help. That's why we're 
here. You've done a really good job of telling me about 
some very difficult material to talk about. One other 
thing I wanted to touch on. A lot of people who try to 
kill themselves, sort of get up the courage to kill 
themselves by drinking. I was wondering if you were 
drinking at all last night. 
Pt.: Not really, maybe a couple of vodkas on the rocks, but 
that's it. I didn't need much alcohol to give me the 
courage to do it. When I reached for the pills, I wanted 
to die, I saw no reason to go on, until I started 
thinking about Bernie again. 
So far the clinician has done a nice job of exploring the presenting 
ideation, always attending to the engagement process, while con¬ 
sciously sticking to the task of creating a verbal videotape of the 
attempt. Powerful information has been shared. And, frankly, it's a 
bit of a "dicey call" at present. Mr. Leland seems to have decided 
against suicide, but the intensity of his pain, the thought-out nature 
of the plan, and the hopelessness of last night (a hopelessness which 
could return quickly) are all disturbing. Clearly, more information 
is needed. Just how extensive have Mr. Leland's thoughts of suicide 

============================================================
PAGE 508 of 792
============================================================
480 Advanced Techniques of Interviewing 
been recently? How close has he been to actually killing himself? 
Let's find out. 
Step 2: Exploration of Recent Suicidal Events 
In this region, the clinician will delineate the types of suicidal 
thoughts and actions that the patient has had during the previous 6 
to 8 weeks. This glimpse of the patient's world can provide im¬ 
portant insights into the degree of suicidal planning and intent that 
the patient has experienced. The more concrete and thorough the 
planning and the more frequent and intense the ideation, the more 
concerned the clinician should be about acute suicide risk. This 
region also provides a wonderful window into the patient's weigh¬ 
ing of the advantages and disadvantages of suicide, including the 
patient's thoughts concerning what death will bring, all of which 
can provide insight into the patient's immediate dangerousness. 
Vague forays such as, "How much have you been thinking 
about suicide?" and "Have you thought about any other ways?"— 
if left alone—are invitations for miscommunication and under-re¬ 
porting of suicidal ideation. With the CASE Approach, this region 
is explored by concretely determining exactly what types of plans 
for suicide the patient has had and how far the patient has acted on 
these plans. Such concrete behavioral information can provide a 
more direct measure of lethality. The best way to approach this 
process unfolds in three steps: (1) Find out what plans have been 
contemplated, (2) determine how far the patient took actions on 
these plans, and (3) determine how much of the patient's time is 
spent on these plans and accompanying ruminations about suicide. 
This database looks somewhat formidable in scope, although it 
is obviously of great potential value. Keep in mind that we are 
trying to gather the most valid and comprehensive database that 
will allow us to make our best educated guess about the patient's 
lethality, because it is always a guess. However, we want to make 
this guess from the best view of "where the patient is at" that we can 
possibly garner. Fortunately, as was the case with our exploration of 
the region of the presenting event, we already have the tools neces¬ 
sary to do the task engagingly and with a minimum of time. Once 
again the behavioral incident will be of great use, but this time it 
will be combined with some of the other validity techniques that 
we discussed in Chapter 7 on uncovering personality dysfunction. 
Specifically, gentle assumption, symptom amplification, and denial 
of the specific will all be utilized. 
The task is rather straightforward. We will delineate the list of 
methods and the extent of action taken upon them by weaving these 
validity techniques into easily remembered strategies. I will talk 

============================================================
PAGE 509 of 792
============================================================
Exploring Suicidal and Homicidal Ideation 481 
about two different approaches, but the reader should feel free to 
design new approaches in a flexible fashion as the needs arise. There 
is no cookbook approach that is correct, but these principles can 
make the exploration of recent ideation and actions surprisingly 
easy and natural. 
The first approach is straightforward. After the clinician has 
finished exploring the region of the presenting event, gentle assump¬ 
tion is used to delineate the next method that was considered by 
the patient, such as the use of a gun. The clinician then proceeds to 
use a series of behavioral incidents to sculpt out how far the patient 
acted on this method. If the patient responds with positive answers 
to each question, the series may look something like this: "Do you 
have a gun in the house?", "Have you ever gotten the gun out with 
the intention of thinking about using it to kill yourself?", "Have 
you ever loaded the gun?", "Have you put the gun up to your body 
or head?", "How long did you hold the gun there?", "Did you take 
the safety off?", "What stopped you from pulling the trigger?" 
Once the clinician has a good idea of how close the patient 
came to killing himself or herself with this method, the clinician 
once again uses gentle assumption to get the third method. This 
method is then explored, using behavioral incidents in exactly the 
same manner. Gentle assumptions are then used with follow-up 
behavioral incidents until the patient denies other methods. 
But the clinician doesn't stop here. Once the use of a gentle 
assumption yields a negative answer, the clinician uses a series of 
denials of the specific. We have seen earlier how surprisingly power¬ 
ful this technique can be, even when a patient has denied sensitive 
material earlier in the interview. The interviewer doesn't drive this 
technique into the ground with a long series of methods, but simply 
asks about any methods that are common to the patient's culture 
and that have not been discussed yet. Thus, if the patient has 
talked about overdosing, guns, and driving the car off the road, the 
clinician may ask the following short list of denials of the specific: 
"Have you thought about cutting or stabbing yourself?", "Have 
you thought about hanging yourself?", "Have you thought about 
jumping off a bridge or other high place?", "Have you thought 
about carbon monoxide?" I never cease to be amazed at how often 
such questions uncover more ideation, and in a few cases I have 
actually had recent attempts uncovered, despite further suicidal 
ideation being denied earlier. As before, if a new method is uncov¬ 
ered the clinician uses a series of behavioral incidents to find out to 
what extent the patient took action. 
This approach for generating the methods and actions taken 
towards suicide is shown in Figure 12. After completing these ex¬ 
plorations, the clinician then hones in on the frequency, duration.

and intensity of the suicidal ideation with a question such as: "Over 
the past 6 to 8 weeks, how much time daily do you think about 
killing yourself, roughly?" 
This approach is logical and simple to remember. It also flows 
imperceptibly for the patient, frequently increasing engagement as 
the patient is pleasantly surprised at how easy it is to talk to the 
clinician about material that had frequently been shouldered as a 
topic of shame for the patient. Let us return to Mr. Leland and see 
if this approach sheds more light into his suicide potential: 
Clin.: You've talked about your son Bernie several times. He 
must mean a lot to you. 
Pt.: He's a good kid. Well, he's not really a kid any more. 
Clin.: How old is he? 
Pt.: Twenty-three and he's gone through a lot. He's right in 
the middle of law school, and he's struggling a bit. 
Then he lost his mom. I don't think he could handle 
his old man killing himself right now. Maybe later. I 
just hope I can hold on for him. 
Clin.: As I hear you talk it's clear you've been in intense 
pain ever since your wife died. How long have you 
been thinking about killing yourself? 
Pt.: That's sort of hard to say. But I thought about killing 
myself the day she died. But I didn't really start 
thinking about it seriously until about 2 months ago. 
Clin.: What other ways, besides pills, have you been 
thinking about (gentle assumption)? 
Pt.: (pause) Well for awhile there I was thinking about 
shooting myself. 
Clin.: Do you have a gun (behavioral incident)? 
Pt.: Not now. I got rid of it. Too dangerous. 
Clin.: How do you mean, too dangerous? 
Pt.: I thought I was going to kill myself with it. 
Clin.: Did you ever get it out with the intention of killing 
yourself (behavioral incident)? 
Pt.: Yes I did. 

============================================================
PAGE 512 of 792
============================================================
484 Advanced Techniques of Interviewing 
Clin.: Did you ever load it with thoughts of killing yourself 
(behavioral incident)? 
Pt.: Yes I did. 
Clin.: What did you do after you loaded it (behavioral 
incident)? 
Pt.: Well I only did that once. I kept the gun in the 
bedroom in the night stand. Bought it for burglars. My 
wife was always worried about burglars (pauses, looks 
away for a moment). Any way, about a month ago, I 
got the thing out. It was a Friday night, some friends 
had invited me over, my friends have been great. But I 
just didn't want to go. I got really bad. And I just 
went to the bed and loaded the thing. 
Clin.: What did you do then (behavioral incident)? 
Pt.: I put it inside my mouth. But I didn't pull the trigger. 
Clin.: Flad you taken off the safety? 
Pt.: Yea. 
Clin.: Wow, close. 
Pt.: Real close. 
Clin.: What stopped you (behavioral incident)? 
Pt.: I don't have the foggiest idea (sighs). I just didn't do 
it. I gave the gun for keeping to a friend the next day. 
If I wouldn't have done that, I really think I would 
have been dead last night. I'd have used it. 
Clin.: That was another example of you stopping yourself. 
What do you make of that? 
Pt.: You know, there must be a part of me that doesn't 
want to do this. Lord knows why. But I've never been 
a quitter. I had a neighbor who killed himself once. 
And I remember thinking it was wrong. Deep in my 
gut I still feel that. I think that's why I'm here today. I 
don't want to kill myself. But the thoughts just keep 
coming. 
Clin.: What other ways have you been thinking about killing 
yourself (gentle assumption)? 
Pt.: Oh I don't think there are any other ways. That's 
enough isn't it (smiles)? 

============================================================
PAGE 513 of 792
============================================================
Exploring Suicidal and Homicidal Ideation 485 
Clin.: Yes, that's enough (both laugh). What about cutting 
yourself or stabbing yourself (denial of the specific)? 
Pt.: That is not appealing, never even crossed my mind. 
Clin.: How about driving your car off the road (denial of the 
specific)? 
Pt.: Thought about it briefly, but it's not a sure thing. 
Clin.: How frequently did you think about it (behavioral 
incident)? 
Pt.: Oh, not often, couple times, just fleetingly. 
Clin.: Did you ever get in the car with the intention of 
killing yourself (behavioral incident)? 
Pt.: No, can't say that I have. 
Clin.: How about hanging yourself (denial of the specific)? 
Pt.: Absolutely no way. Never crossed my mind. That's 
repellent to me. 
Clin.: How about carbon monoxide (denial of the specific)? 
Pt.: You know, I did think of that. Back when I was 
thinking of shooting myself, I also thought about that. 
Clin.: Did you go to the car and check it out with thoughts 
of doing that (behavioral incident)? 
Pt.: Sort of. 
Clin.: What do you mean, what did you do (behavioral 
incident)? 
Pt.: I went out to the garage and sort of looked things 
over. I wanted to see if there were doors I'd need to 
seal up and cracks in the walls. I even looked at my 
hose, but couldn't figure out how you are supposed to 
attach the damn thing to your exhaust. 
Clin.: What did you do next (behavioral incident)? 
Pt.: Nothing (smiles sort of sheepishly). I thought it would 
be too much damn trouble (shakes his head from side 
to side and smiles again in embarrassment). 
Clin.: Over the past couple of months how much time have 
you been spending thinking about all these ways, say 
on a daily basis (behavioral incident)? 

============================================================
PAGE 514 of 792
============================================================
486 Advanced Techniques of Interviewing 
Pt.: That's hard to say. 
Clin.: Couple of hours a day: 4, 6 hours, most of the day 
(symptom amplification)? 
Pt.: Here's the strange thing. It's actually gotten better. 
Back when I was thinking about the gun, I was 
sometimes thinking about it most of the day. But it's 
been much less, not even daily and then boom, I just 
felt like crap last night. It was sort of strange. But I 
really don't want to kill myself now. If I'd wanted to, I 
could have done it last night after they let me go 
home, but I don't really want to. I'm really not a weak 
person. Really. 
Clin.: I can see that. And I think we have a lot to offer you. 
Pt.: Good. I've heard very good things about this place. 
This has proved to be a powerful segment of interviewing, done 
nicely. Much has come to light. In the first place there are some 
promising signs here. Mr. Leland clearly seems to be denying current 
intent, and it comes across in a believable way. He also had a 
weapon removed from his house, which shows a real instinct for 
self-protection. He seems to have a strong superego, and this is 
helping him to feel that it is important to avoid suicide. The frame¬ 
work for meaning, concerning his son, sounds powerful. Ironically, 
it is given even more validity by his comment that maybe he would 
commit suicide later but not now, because his son really couldn't 
handle it now. This suggests that he has really given a lot of thought 
to his son's needs and seems committed "to living." 
Also, notice that his affect has improved as the suicide assess¬ 
ment has progressed. There is some genuine humor there, and he is 
building a bond with the interviewer. This could prove to be an 
important point in helping him to remain as an outpatient, perhaps 
in a crisis support group. 
On the other hand, the clinician has deftly uncovered a signifi¬ 
cant background of suicidal ideation and some significant action, 
which some clinicians might have missed. Mr. Leland's suicide risk 
is to be taken quite seriously. I believe that he could very well have 
killed himself if he had not disposed of the gun. Moreover, if Mr. 
Leland were to demonstrate any "gaminess" about his safety in the 
rest of the interview or look more ambivalent, the clinician now has 
enough background information so that he would probably lean 
towards hospitalization, even commitment. Note that the careful 
and persistent use of gentle assumptions and behavioral incidents 
has led to some very strong grounds for an involuntary commit- 

============================================================
PAGE 515 of 792
============================================================
Exploring Suicidal and Homicidal Ideation 487 
ment, which "would stick" if needed. A judge is much more likely 
to be impressed with the "gun in the mouth" than the relatively 
small overdose. 
The excerpt also demonstrates a beneficial but rather peculiar 
"side effect" of using behavioral incidents to find out how far a 
person has taken actions towards self-harm. Not infrequently, while 
patients are describing their actions, they spontaneously discuss 
some of the advantages and disadvantages about killing themselves. 
This listing often provides important clues with regard to which 
"way they are leaning." For instance, in the above excerpt, while 
discussing why he stopped himself and did not proceed to use the 
gun, Mr. Leland raised the issue of a friend who had killed himself 
and we uncovered his bias that suicide is morally weak, which 
represents an important deterrent from his perspective. 
Sometimes, at such moments, it is an ideal time for the inter¬ 
viewer to lead the dialogue into a deeper exploration as to what the 
patient hopes to achieve by his or her own death. In his wonderfully 
insightful and very practical book. Suicide Risk: The Formulation of 
Clinical Judgment, John T. Maltsberger points out the diversity of 
fashions in which patients can view their own death by suicide: 
1. Suicide is a gateway leading into a dreamless sleep (nothingness). 
2. It will effect reunion with someone or something that has been 
lost. 
3. It will be a way of escaping from a persecutory enemy, interior 
or exterior. 
4. It will destroy an enemy who seems to have taken up a place in 
the patient's body or some other part of himself. 
5. It will provide a passage into another, better world. 
6. One can get revenge on someone else by abandoning him or by 
destroying his favorite possession (the patient's body), and one 
can then watch him suffer from beyond the grave.38 
The clinician can ask questions that explore regions such as the 
above, and the answers can provide insight into the drives motiva¬ 
ting the individual patient towards death, perhaps gaining further 
insight into the imminent risk of a suicide attempt. These beliefs 
can serve as surprisingly powerful, and sometimes odd, motivators 
for suicide, especially in people who see no other ways of resolving 
their current pain or who find existence, as we know it, to be a 
bleak proposition. This was highlighted in the mass suicide of 39 
people in a rich suburb of San Diego. The idea that suicide could 
provide a method of passage to a better world was the driving force 
behind the members of the Heaven's Gate cult. The cult members 
believed that their death would carry them to a better world aboard 

============================================================
PAGE 516 of 792
============================================================
488 Advanced Techniques of Interviewing 
an alien spacecraft, which just happened to be tagging a ride inside 
the tail of the Hale-Bopp Comet. 
I had mentioned there are other ways to approach the task of 
exploring the region of recent suicidal events. In the second most 
popular way that I have seen (and this is a way that I'm very 
comfortable with as well), the clinician first generates the entire list 
of suicide methods, contemplated by the patient, and then goes back 
to explore each one in detail. 
In this approach, after the clinician has finished exploring the 
presenting method, gentle assumption is used to find out the second 
method, if indeed one has been considered. In the first approach, 
the clinician would have proceeded to use behavioral incidents to 
delineate the extent of action taken on this new method, but this is 
not the case in this approach. As soon as the second method is 
uncovered, the clinician asks another gentle assumption such as, 
"What other ways have you thought of killing yourself?" This use 
of gentle assumptions is continued until the patient answers that 
there are no other ways. The clinician does not stop there. The 
interviewer proceeds to use denial of the specific repeatedly until 
the list is done. It might be useful to look at Figure 13 now, because 
it shows this approach in detail, making it easy to see how it differs 
from the first approach that we discussed. 
At this point the clinician then returns to each method in turn 
and uses a string of behavioral incidents to find out how far the 
patient has gone in taking actions towards self-harm. For instance, 
the clinician might say, using an internal referred gate, "Earlier you 
mentioned that you had thought of shooting yourself. Is there a gun 
in the house or one available to you?" A series of clarifying behav¬ 
ioral incidents would follow, concerning the actions taken with the 
gun. When the interviewer has completed his or her determination 
of the extent of action taken by the patient on this second suicide 
method, the interviewer will proceed to use the exact same tactic 
with the third method, and so forth. 
Just as with the first strategy, after all methods are explored 
with behavioral incidents, the clinician checks on the frequency, 
duration, and intensity of the recent ideation with a question such 
as, "Looking at all these methods combined, over the past 6 to 8 
weeks, how much of any given day are you spending on thoughts 
of suicide?" 
Both methods are easy to remember. In the first strategy, as 
each method is uncovered, the clinician uses behavioral incidents to 
follow the extent of action taken. In the second method, a list of all 
the methods is uncovered first before any of them are explored in 
detail with behavioral incidents. You can try both methods and 
decide which one you prefer, or you can develop entirely new
