APPENDIX IV 
TIPS FOR PASSING THE ORAL 
BOARDS IN PSYCHIATRY 
We will begin with understatement. The Oral Boards are not a lot 
of fun. This is one of the few statements, which I have ever uttered, 
that has met with universal agreement. The Boards are a "rite of 
passage" for most psychiatrists: They are always memorable one 
way or another, and there is a great feeling of relief once one 
passes them. 
In this appendix I will pass on some tips that helped me to 
pass the Boards and have also helped many of the candidates whom 
I have assisted with their preparation for the Boards. This appendix 
is not a replacement for a sound source of overall information about 
the Boards. The reader should carefully read everything that the 
Board sends you and also practice a lot. A great source of practical 
advice and background can be found in the book Boarding Time, 2nd 
edition, by James Morrison and Rodrigo Munoz (APA Press, 1996). 
The coaching tips are described under five categories: Successful 
Attitude, What is Tested, Interview Tips, Presentation Tips, and 
Preparation Suggestions. 
Successful Attitude 
One of the biggest hurdles I find, with clinicians having problems 
with the Boards, arises from an unnecessarily intense fear of the 
Boards themselves, often generated by a false belief. This anxiety- 
provoking belief sounds one way or another something like the 
following statement, "The Boards are extremely difficult and artifi¬ 
cial. They don't look like any type of clinical interview that I do." 
Don't say this to yourself, because it is very anxiety-producing 
and suggests that the examinee is being tested on a technique that 
is unfamiliar. This concept is indeed a frightening thought. Avoid 
it—it is very counterproductive. 
But there is a more important reason to avoid this belief, other 
than that it is counterproductive. It is patently false. You have 
successfully performed a similar 30-minute interview many times in 
your training, under extremely difficult circumstances, and you have 
done it well. You have carried out this style of interview when you 
have been "beat tired," harried, and under incredible time pressures. 
715 

============================================================
PAGE 744 of 792
============================================================
716 Appendix IV 
You have done this style of interview successfully many times, when 
an error does not result in someone failing a test but can result in 
someone losing his or her life. You have successfully and gracefully 
done this interview many times, because you have done a very 
similar interview every single time that you have been "on call." 
You see, in many respects the "Board interview" is an emergency 
room interview with a few extras. 
When you enter the interview room, don't focus on passing a 
test. Focus on doing a good clinical interview, much like you have 
done scores of times in the emergency room. Tell yourself, "I've 
done this before and I know how to do it." Do a good clinical 
interview, and you will have done a good "Board interview." 
The APBN is trying to ensure that practicing psychiatrists are 
safe and can soundly perform key clinical skills such as inter¬ 
viewing, case formulation, and treatment planning. They are not 
trying to see if you can do a comprehensive 60-minute intake in 30 
minutes. The Board is attempting to determine whether the inter¬ 
viewer can gather a reasonable database in a 30-minute time period 
that results in a sound formulation and disposition. They do not 
expect you to do the impossible, but they do expect you to appropri¬ 
ately structure the patient towards giving pertinent information. In 
a nutshell they want to see if examinees are facile at engagement, 
practical in choosing areas to explore, and safe in judgment. These 
skills are exactly the ones that you have used numerous times in 
the emergency room. 
What is Tested 
As you are probably already aware, the examination has two 
parts—a live patient examination and a videotape examination. In 
the live patient format, the clinician interviews the patient for 30 
minutes and then has 30 minutes to present the patient data and 
formulation and answer the examiner's questions. In the videotape 
format, you will watch a 30-minute videotape and then have 30 
minutes to present the case and your formulation as well as answer¬ 
ing the questions of the examiners. With both of these formats, the 
actual case presentation usually takes about 5 to 10 minutes with the 
rest of the 30 minutes devoted to questions posed by the examiners. 
In the live interview format, the actual grading subcategories 
are as follows: 
1. Physician-patient relationship 
2. Conduct of the interview 
3. Organization and presentation of data 
4. Phenomenology, diagnosis, and prognosis 

============================================================
PAGE 745 of 792
============================================================
Tips for Passing the Oral Boards in Psychiatry 717 
5. Etiologic, pathogenic, and therapeutic issues (divided into biolog¬ 
ical, psychological, and social categories) 
The subcategories for the videotape are quite similar. Note that 
although there is no interview for them to grade, they are quite 
interested in your observations about the patient from the videotape, 
which you will be presenting in the mental status assessment and 
in your discussion: 
1. Observation of data 
2. Organization and presentation of data 
3. Phenomenology, diagnosis, and prognosis 
4. Etiologic, pathogenic, and therapeutic issues (divided as above) 
I believe that one of the most important points to take from the 
above listing is the fact that, although the actual interview is quite 
important, much of the grading is done on other fields of evaluation, 
such as your presentation skills, treatment planning, and core clini¬ 
cal knowledge base. 
Pay particular attention to the role of the videotape examina¬ 
tion. All of us have been trained for some time in performing 
interviews and then presenting formulations. Almost none of us 
have been trained, nor practiced, watching a videotape, culling 
data from it, and then creating a presentation from the video data. 
However, you are being tested on this very set of skills. Practice. By 
the time that you reach the exam room you should feel comfortable 
with culling data and making presentations from watching a video. 
It is easy to do once you get used to it, but it feels surprisingly 
different compared with actively developing the database yourself. 
One of the main reasons why I think that clinicians fluster and 
fail the examination is that they do not allocate enough time to 
practice culling data from a videotape. I suggest that you should 
watch at least five 30-minute videos; spend 5 minutes formulating 
your presentation after each one; and then present your case presen¬ 
tation without interruption. Practice your presentation exactly as it 
will occur in the Boards. If you don't feel comfortable at the end of 
five videos, do more. Interviewing videos are available as commer¬ 
cial educational products and at some medical libraries and aca¬ 
demic centers. Psychiatric residencies may also have tapes available 
for training purposes. 
Interview Tips 
Make no bones about it, time is tight during the Boards, but no 
tighter than in a busy inner city emergency room (clinicians some- 

============================================================
PAGE 746 of 792
============================================================
718 Appendix IV 
times have less than 30 minutes in these conditions). However, you 
already have an understanding of how to successfully create an 
engaging and sound Board interview if you understand the princi¬ 
ples explored in this book. 
Just as you do with any other clinical interview, using your 
understanding of facilics and time utilization, before you even begin 
the interview, you should enter with an understanding of which 
topical regions need to be explored and how much material you 
need to explore in each region. Once within the interview, you start 
with an open-ended and engaging scouting phase lasting about 5 
minutes and then proceed to gather your data region by region. 
When each region is completed, you gracefully use natural and 
referred gates to enter the next region and complete it to your 
satisfaction. If a patient begins to unnecessarily pivot out of a given 
region, you gently pull the patient back. If the patient shows stylistic 
resistances, such as being shut-down or wandering, you use the 
same techniques for transforming these resistances as already de¬ 
scribed in Chapter 2. 
The skills used in the Board interview are identical to your 
daily interviewing skills. The Board interview is a clinical interview. 
As with other time-limited interviews, such as an ER interview, the 
key is that the clinician understands exactly how much material is 
not sought compared with a standard 60-minute intake. In short, 
the interviewer needs a very concrete idea of what he or she is not 
going to ask about in each region. 
By way of example, let's take a look at the Family History. In a 
standard 60-minute intake, this is explored thoroughly. In an ER, 
and at the Boards, this region is reduced significantly. In the Board- 
style interview, the clinician will ask only about major psychiatric 
disorders (e.g., schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, depression, anxiety 
disorders, substance abuse). The clinician will want to know about 
any suicides in the family. That's it. That's all there is time for. There 
is no time to explore for more subtle diagnoses such as cyclothymic 
disorder or dysthymic disorder and minimal time for details about 
treatment interventions for family members. 
If any of this "unexplored" material may be relevant to treat¬ 
ment planning, this can easily be addressed in your formulation as 
follows, "I am a bit suspicious that my patient may have some 
bipolar type II propensities. One thing I would do when,I saw this 
patient in my next session would be to do a more thorough Family 
History, specifically looking for evidence of cyclothymic disorder or 
bipolar type II process," or "As I stated, I believe my patient has a 
panic disorder. One thing I did not have time to explore in detail in 
this interview was whether or not her mother had a true panic 
disorder. In my next session I would explore this in more detail and 

============================================================
PAGE 747 of 792
============================================================
Tips for Passing the Oral Boards in Psychiatry 719 
also find out which med seemed to help her mother and at what 
doses, for this might point me towards the best med to help my 
current patient." Board examiners will love such awareness of what 
is important to gather "next time," because it shows sound clinical 
thinking, which is what the Boards are all about. 
REGIONS TO EXPLORE 
From our understanding of facilics, we know that the success of a 
time-limited interview depends directly on how clear the clinician 
is regarding what specific information he or she wants to obtain in 
each region of the interview. Bearing this principle in mind, let's 
take a look at which regions need to be explored in a typical Board 
examination. These regions are not listed, necessarily, in any special 
order. As with all clinical interviews, the clinician decides the order 
that best fits with the defenses and needs of the specific patient, 
each of whom is unique. 
I. The following regions should be explored carefully, essentially 
exactly as you would explore them in a standard 60-minute 
assessment: 
1. Patient identification, reason for presentation (e.g., what 
brought the patient into the hospital or clinic), and chief 
complaint 
2. History of the present illness of the suspected primary diag¬ 
nosis (either Axis I or Axis II) 
3. Suicide/violence assessment (the CASE Approach can be 
very useful here) 
II. The following symptom regions should be explored relatively 
carefully: 
1. Mood disturbance symptoms 
2. Anxiety disorder symptoms 
3. Psychotic symptoms 
4. Drug and alcohol symptoms 
5. Disorders due to a medical condition, such as dementia 
6. Other diagnoses that may appear likely, such as eating disor¬ 
ders, adjustment disorders, or personality disorders 
7. Medical history and medical ROS 
This does not mean that you are expected necessarily to expand all 
the diagnoses under each specific symptom cluster. Instead, you 
want to be certain to enter all of these diagnostic regions at some 
point during the interview, ask some probe questions, and then 
decide if a specific diagnosis is likely, at which point you would 
explore that specific diagnosis more carefully with DSM-IV criteria. 

============================================================
PAGE 748 of 792
============================================================
720 Appendix IV 
Keep in mind that the examiners want to see if you can intelligently 
probe in all major areas of psychopathology, wisely choose which 
areas to expand in more detail, and ultimately defend your deci¬ 
sions. Your goal is to be able to present a good differential diagnosis 
in your formulation, not necessarily to have identified exactly every 
diagnosis. 
III. You should definitely touch upon the following regions, but they 
are significantly less thoroughly explored than in a standard 60- 
minute interview: 
1. Past psychiatric history 
2. Family history 
3. Social and developmental history 
4. Cognitive mental status (the noncognitive aspects of the men¬ 
tal status are explored and observed throughout the interview 
as you would normally do) 
By way of example, let us look at the cognitive section of the mental 
status evaluation. The examinee should always address the cognitive 
examination at some point in the interview. A nice selection of probe 
questions could include orientation, digit spans, and three-word 
recall. That is all that is necessary unless the patient appears to be 
suffering from a specific cognitive disorder (e.g., delirium or demen¬ 
tia), in which case one would devote more time during the interview 
to the cognitive examination. 
Remember, even if you happen to leave something out of the 
interview, which you regret omitting, because it ultimately proved 
to be relevant to your formulation, all is not lost. During your 
presentation simply say something like, "Because of the time con¬ 
straints of this setting, I did not do a thorough cognitive exam. But 
I have become suspicious of the presence of a mild dementia and, 
in actual practice, I would perform a more thorough cognitive exam, 
including perhaps a Folstein Mini-Mental Status." Such an explana¬ 
tion would probably satisfy most examiners. 
Perhaps the biggest reduction in the amount of data gathered 
in a Board examination, as opposed to a 60-minute assessment, is in 
the social history. As with an ER exam, past social history is cut 
markedly. 
I will now list some good probe questions which, time permit¬ 
ting, you can follow up on. If these, or similar, questions are asked, 
the candidate will probably gather enough social history to present 
some of the social and psychological factors that help us to under¬ 
stand the patient in a psychosocial context. At a minimum, the 
questions will probably dig up some material that you can tell the 
examiners in your formulation are ripe areas for further exploration 
in future sessions. Keep in mind that during the unstructured scout- 

============================================================
PAGE 749 of 792
============================================================
Tips for Passing the Oral Boards in Psychiatry 721 
ing phase, at the beginning of the interview, patients will also 
frequently reveal important social and psychodynamic issues. 
1. Tell me a little bit about what it was like growing up in your fam- 
iiy. 
2. Tell me a little bit about what school was like for you. 
3. What are some of the jobs you've had, and what have they been 
like for you? 
4. Have you ever had any troubles with the law? 
5. Tell me a little about your wife (substitute words such as signifi¬ 
cant other, boyfriend, girlfriend for "wife" as appropriate). 
You might not even have time for all of these probe questions, 
therefore just do your best with the time that you have available. If 
you suspect earlier in the interview that a personality disorder is 
playing a prominent role in the patient's presentation—especially if 
you think that it is the primary diagnosis—then you will have 
needed to have expanded the diagnostic criteria for this disorder 
fairly carefully, usually during the differential diagnostic component 
of the interview performed earlier. Such primary personality expan¬ 
sions will inevitably provide even further pertinent social history. 
By the way, you don't need to cover all the criteria, you simply 
should be prepared to discuss your differential diagnosis on Axis II 
and be able to defend your reasoning. For instance, "With regard to 
Axis II, I did not have time to gather enough historical information 
to make a definite diagnosis, but I am suspicious of an antisocial 
personality disorder. My evidence for this is as follows. In the HPI, 
he described frequent problems with the law as well as . . . etc." 
On the other hand, personality pathology may not show itself 
until the social history itself is being explored. If such is the case 
some simple personality screening can be attempted here in the 
social history, as time permits, enough to give you food for discus¬ 
sion when presenting Axis II. Remember that on Axis II you can 
discuss personality traits and/or defense mechanisms as well. 
TIME SUGGESTIONS 
There is no predetermined "right way" to allot time during the 
Boards, but here is one strategy. Once again these phases are not 
presented in any fixed order, other than to start with the scouting 
phase, although most people do like to follow the scouting phase 
with the HPI and DSM-IV differential diagnosis: 
• 5 Minutes Scouting Phase (Including ID, Chief Complaint, 
and Reason for Evaluation. Note that the 
patient may spontaneously provide much of the 
HPI here.) 

============================================================
PAGE 750 of 792
============================================================
722 Appendix IV 
•12 Minutes 
• 4 Minutes 
• 3 Minutes 
• 3 Minutes 
• 2 Minutes 
• 1 Minute 
HPI, DSM-IV differential diagnoses, and 
assessment of suicidal/violent ideation 
Social History (may include some simple 
personality exploration) 
Medical History, Medical ROS, and Family 
History 
Cognitive Mental Status 
Past Psychiatric History 
Closing (Be sure to tell the patient that he or she 
did a good job and to thank the patient for 
participating.) 
Good luck with the interview! If you practice the above principles 
diligently, I think you will be pleasantly surprised at the large 
amount of data that can be sensitively generated in 30 minutes, if 
the clinician has a sound understanding of the facilics of the task. 
Interestingly, you may discover (also to your pleasant surprise) that 
your practice for the Boards will have made you a better interviewer 
in the ER as well. 
Presentation Tips 
In some respects, the candidate's ability to present his or her case 
data and formulation may represent the single most important skill 
to practice. A candidate who presents with confidence and organiza¬ 
tion will project competence. A candidate who presents haltingly or 
in a disorganized manner will invite failure. Don't deceive yourself; 
first impressions count. A skilled presenter creates an expectation 
among the examiners that they are about to see someone who will 
pass. Everything said from that point onward has the benefit of this 
positive "lens." On the other hand, a weak presentation creates a 
negative "filter" through which the examiners unconsciously may 
be looking for the worse. 
For this reason alone, it is wise to practice presenting frequently. 
However, there is another reason. Clinicians interview daily. Clini¬ 
cians do not present daily. It is surprising how poorly some psychia¬ 
trists are trained in presentation skills, and residencies vary widely 
regarding how well this skill is taught. In the Boards, you will have 
only about 5 minutes to prepare your presentation after interviewing 
the patient or watching the video. Believe me—the 5 minutes pass 
quickly. 
Presenting a case is not particularly difficult, if one has practiced 
the process a lot. Some people will have been "through the mill" in 
this regard because of an emphasis upon it in their training. They 

============================================================
PAGE 751 of 792
============================================================
Tips for Passing the Oral Boards in Psychiatry 723 
will not need to practice this skill too much, but even these exami¬ 
nees should practice presenting at least several times in addition to 
doing their mock Boards. 
If your presenting skills are not strong, I urge you to practice 
interviewing a patient or watching a video at least 10 to 15 times, 
followed by a 5-minute organization time, and then present the case 
to a mirror or to a colleague. Another way to practice your presenta¬ 
tion skills is to take advantage of the fact that you are probably 
doing assessments every day in your actual clinic work. Occasion¬ 
ally, instead of dictating your assessment right away, dictate it "after 
hours." Instead, as soon as you are done with one of your clinic 
assessments, organize for 5 minutes and then present directly to a 
colleague or to a mirror non-stop. It's a great way to practice. It 
doesn't matter that this was a 60-minute assessment: It's the skills 
of organizing your data rapidly and presenting smoothly that you 
are practicing. 
When practicing your presentations, do not stop. If you make 
a mistake or pause awkwardly, continue. You must become adept at 
smoothly covering such errors while maintaining a calm composure. 
Also, keep in mind that examiners are probably more comfortable 
with your pauses in presentation than you are. They realize that 
sometimes we need a moment to organize our thoughts. There 
is nothing wrong with this process as long as you don't appear 
flustered. 
Concerning time, your presentation should probably last about 
5 to 10 minutes at most. Organization is critical both for keeping 
you calm and for looking like you know what you are talking about. 
The Board does not officially provide a guide for the outline of the 
presentation, but the following may be useful. In it, the examinee 
first provides the database, then proceeds to present a diagnostic 
formulation, and ends by presenting further labs to be considered 
and treatment modalities to be utilized. It looks as follows: 
I. Database 
A. Identification and Chief Complaint 
B. History of the Present Illness 
C. Pertinent Past Psychiatric History 
D. Pertinent Social History 
E. Pertinent Family History 
F. Medical History and Review of Symptoms 
G. Mental Status 
1. Appearance and Behavior 
2. Speech and Thought Process 
3. Mood and Affect 
4. Perceptual Abnormalities 
5. Thought Content and Lethality 
6. Sensorium and Cognitive Findings 

============================================================
PAGE 752 of 792
============================================================
724 Appendix IV 
II. Diagnostic Formulation 
A. DSM-IV Differential Diagnosis 
Review all five axes of the DSM-IV in order, listing the 
most likely diagnoses and rule-outs. (Be sure to mention 
any organic disorders that may need to be ruled out.) 
B. Psychosocial/Psychodynamic Formulation 
Nothing fancy here—there is no need for "name throw¬ 
ing" of theorists, etc. Just briefly mention any pressing 
psychosocial and psychological conflicts/defenses that 
you think are important. Focus on issues that will play a 
direct role in treatment intervention. If the examiners want 
more, they will ask for it. 
III. Treatment Planning 
A. For each diagnosis list your possible treatment plans and 
modalities. Be sure to include any future labs or testing 
that you think are needed. If there are specific psychosocial 
problems, include your tentative interventions such as case 
management, help with housing, psychoeducation for family 
members, and so forth. 
The treatment planning schema, based on systems the¬ 
ory presented in Chapter 4, can be very useful here. Indeed, 
another way to present the treatment interventions is to 
simply go from one level to another through this schema. In 
short, list the treatment interventions that you are consider¬ 
ing in the following systems: biological interventions (e.g., 
meds, labwork, referral to internist), psychological interven¬ 
tions (e.g., individual psychotherapies, readings, hotlines), 
interpersonal interventions (e.g., social skills training), family 
interventions (e.g., family therapies, psychoeducation, home 
outreach, referral to a local NAMI chapter), and social or 
community interventions (e.g., case management, vocational 
rehabilitation, support groups, AA). 
Practicing your presentations, while using the above guidelines, 
should help you to present fluidly and concisely, while also increas¬ 
ing your sense of self-confidence. I also suggest that you literally 
introduce each area as you come to it. For instance, "Concerning the 
HPI, Mr. Janson reports his symptoms first began ..." When done 
with the HPI, one would continue, "Concerning the patient's Past 
Psychiatric History, the pertinent positives are a hospitalization for 
schizophrenia at age 18 and a subsequent . . . ." This framing of 
each section has a nice professional feel to it, and it also helps the 
presenter to maintain a focused organization. 
Be prepared for the fact that some examiners like to interrupt 
the presentation with questions. If this occurs, simply answer the 

============================================================
PAGE 753 of 792
============================================================
Tips for Passing the Oral Boards in Psychiatry 725 
questions and then return to your presentation. Never look discon¬ 
certed by examiner interruptions. Answer openly and with interest. 
Never bullshit. This will only get you into trouble. 
In fact, sometimes it is advantageous to openly admit what you 
don't know and what you would do about that deficit in real 
practice. Sound clinicians are recognizable by the degree with which 
they know what they don't know. By way of example, the following 
admission would probably impress board examiners, "I'm not en¬ 
tirely certain whether or not the Paxil would impact on the blood 
level of Serax. Before I would prescribe Serax, I would definitely 
look that up in the PDR. If I wasn't satisfied there, I would call the 
pharmacist. But no matter what, I always check into drug-drug 
interactions before I prescribe." 
Examinees often want to know what type of questions are fair 
game. The examiners are asked to focus their questioning on practi¬ 
cal clinical issues that are raised by your case or could be extended 
into general practice. They are to avoid trivia. They are not trying 
to stump you. They simply want to see what you know and whether 
or not you apply your knowledge in a competent fashion. The 
examiners are urged to help decrease undue stress. In this regard, 
the examiner instructions state, "It is the examiner's job to help the 
candidate to display competence in what might be best described 
as an environment of compassionate collegial neutrality." These 
examiners remember what it was like to take this test, and they are 
there to help you do your best. 
Preparation Suggestions 
For your part, "doing your best" requires three things: (1) Practice, 
(2) Practice, and (3) Practice. Unfamiliarity with the protocol, and a 
subsequent freezing up during the examination, is probably the 
prime reason why competent psychiatrists sometimes fail the 
boards. This factor can essentially be eliminated by practice. It also 
helps to remember, as stated before, that you have often done a 
similar type of interview in the ER. 
It is critical that you undergo enough mock Boards to feel 
comfortable with the protocol and atmosphere of the examinations 
themselves. Many residencies are now doing a good job of providing 
mock Boards. If some time has elapsed since you did your mock 
Boards, or if you want more practice, get a colleague to function as 
an examiner. Make sure that the colleague acts exactly like an 
examiner, showing no familiarity with you or your work and follow¬ 
ing the time schedule of the Boards to a tee. It is best to have two 
colleagues acting as examiners, just as will occur in the Boards. 
In addition to doing mock Boards, as noted earlier, I would 

============================================================
PAGE 754 of 792
============================================================
726 Appendix IV 
repeatedly practice developing and presenting a case from a 30- 
minute video, as well as other methods of practicing your presenta¬ 
tions mentioned earlier. Board review courses can be very valuable, 
but make sure that the course you choose includes opportunities for 
both your interview and presentation to be directly watched and 
feedback given. For more background and practical tips for passing 
the Boards, as mentioned earlier. Boarding Time, by Morrison and 
Munoz is an outstanding book. Naturally, you should also do some 
refresher reading, especially with regard to DSM-IV differential di¬ 
agnosis and clinical pharmacology. 
In closing this Appendix, I hope that the above tips prove to be 
of some value. Trust in yourself and your skills. With good prepara¬ 
tion, sound clinicians tend to do well on the Boards. Good Luck 
and Good Boarding! 

============================================================
PAGE 755 of 792
============================================================
Glossary of Interview 
Supervision Terms 
Blending 
Blending is the subjective and objective evidence, collected from 
the interview, which suggests that the engagement process is going 
well. For group discussion and supervision purposes, it is useful to 
rate blending on a scale from zero (patient is hostile toward clini¬ 
cian) to ten (patient and clinician appear extremely natural together). 
The degree of blending can be determined from three complemen¬ 
tary perspectives: (1) the clinician's subjective feelings, (2) objective 
evidence from the patient's behavior (e.g., increased eye contact or 
smiling), and (3) self-report from the patient. The degree of blending 
depends on two major elements—the clinician's skill and the 
patient's defenses and psychopathology. Consequently, low blending 
is not necessarily a sign of poor clinician performance. For instance, 
a skilled clinician may only achieve a blending of three with a 
patient who displays active paranoid process. 
Closed-Ended Questions 
Closed-ended questions are those questions that are extremely 
easy for a moderately guarded or moderately resistant patient to 
answer with one word, a short phrase, or a simple "yes" or "no." 
All inquiries that provide or imply a specific list of answers or ask 
for specific items, places, dates, numbers, or names are viewed as 
closed-ended. In patients who are easily engaged, these questions 
may also tend to decrease interviewee response length, for in many 
instances the socially appropriate answer is only one word, a short 
phrase, or a simple "yes" or "no." 
Content Region 
An area of dialogue in which a specific topic becomes the center 
of focus. Typical content regions include diagnostic regions, history 
of the present illness, social history, family history, and the lethality 
assessment. 
727 

============================================================
PAGE 756 of 792
============================================================
728 Glossary of Interview Supervision Terms 
Engagement 
The actual degree to which the patient feels comfortable and 
safe with interviewer. It is reflected in the "blending" of the inter¬ 
view. 
Facilics 
Facilics is the study of the flow and structuring of the inter¬ 
viewing process, including the study of the clinician's time manage¬ 
ment. In its applied format it includes the study of the methods 
utilized by clinicians to explore specific topics and processes (deline¬ 
ated as "regions") as well as the study of transitions (delineated as 
"gates") between these regions. A method of diagramming the vari¬ 
ous regions and transitions has been developed and is currently 
utilized as both a supervision and research tool. In a pure research 
form, facilic analysis can be applied to any dialogue, whether an 
everyday conversation or a television interview. 
Gentle Assumption 
A technique for increasing the likelihood that sensitive material 
will be discussed more openly. The clinician assumes that the sus¬ 
pected behavior is occurring and frames a question based on this 
assumption. Thus, the clinician does not ask, "Do you masturbate?" 
but "How frequently do you masturbate during a typical week, if 
at all?" 
Gentle Commands 
Gentle commands represent one of the two most powerful 
open-ended techniques (open-ended questions being the other). 
They begin with phrases such as "Tell me about your . . ." or "Say 
something about . . which gently direct the patient to discuss a 
topic. The tone of voice is gentle and nonthreatening. An example 
of such a statement is "Tell me something about your stress on 
the job." 
* 
Implied Gate 
A relatively smooth transition initiated by the clinician, who 
leaves one topic by discussing a closely related topic that seems to 
follow the conversation. It does not (as in the case of the natural 
gate) take its cue from the immediately preceding patient response. 

============================================================
PAGE 757 of 792
============================================================
Glossary of Interview Supervision Terms 729 
Introduced Gate 
These gates do not naturally follow the flow of conversation, 
but they are relatively smooth, for the clinician verbally notes that 
a transition is under way An example is as follows: "For a moment 
let's change topics to how your mood has been recently." 
Manufactured Gate 
A technique for setting up a natural transition by bringing up 
a bridging topic. For instance, let us say that the clinician had 
finished an expansion of major depression and wanted to enter the 
region of alcoholism. The clinician might say, "Earlier you had 
mentioned your father had drinking problems, how about yourself, 
do you drink much?" Manufactured gates are actually combination 
gates. This example began as a referred gate into family history, but 
the clinician immediately used a natural gate to enter the region of 
alcohol dependence. There is usually a smoother and simpler way 
to make a transition than a manufactured gate. 
Natural Expansion 
An exploration of a given topic in which the clinician's ques¬ 
tions seem to follow naturally and convey an almost conversational 
feeling to the interview. 
Natural Gate 
A smooth transition from one topic to a new topic initiated by 
the clinician. In a natural gate the clinician cues directly off the 
patient's immediately preceding statement. 
Open-Ended Questions 
Open-ended questions are difficult to answer with one word or 
a short phrase, even if the interviewee is moderately guarded or 
moderately resistant. It is virtually impossible to respond to these 
questions with a simple "yes" or "no." Questions that provide or 
imply possible answers, or ask for specific items, places, dates, 
numbers, or names, are never viewed as open-ended, because they 
limit the freedom of answer choice. In patients who are easily 
engaged, open-ended questions should tend to produce relatively 
large quantities of speech. 

============================================================
PAGE 758 of 792
============================================================
730 Glossary of Interview Supervision Terms 
PACE 
An acronym representing the four major assessments made by 
a clinician during the scouting period: 
P = Patient's perspective and conscious agenda 
A = Assessment of the patient's mental status on an informal 
basis 
C = Clinician's perception of the patient's problems and uncon¬ 
scious agenda 
E = Evaluation of the interview process itself (e.g., is the inter¬ 
view shut down, wandering, or rehearsed?) 
Phantom Gate 
This relatively weak transition occurs when a new topic is 
introduced with an abrupt entrance that seems to come from no¬ 
where. Such gates may convey a sense of awkwardness or disinterest 
on the part of the clinician, especially if his or her style is frequently 
characterized by such blunt transitions. 
Pivot Points 
A pivot point occurs when a patient spontaneously changes 
topics. If the clinician is consciously aware of a pivot point, then a 
decision can be made regarding the wisdom of following or gently 
refocusing the patient. Uncontrolled wandering interviews fre¬ 
quently result when clinicians miss pivot points and consequently 
never appropriately focus the patient. 
Process Regions 
An area of dialogue in which the focus is on the manner in 
which the patient answers, not the content of what is said. Classic 
process regions include facilatory regions, psychodynamic regions, 
and regions in which specific types of resistance are addressed. 
Other process regions include phenomenological inquiries and edu¬ 
cational regions. Sometimes process regions may also function as 
content regions, as is the case with phenomenological and educa¬ 
tional regions. 
Referred Gate 
A graceful transition in which a new topic is entered by refer¬ 
ring to a previous topic raised earlier in the interview. These transi- 

============================================================
PAGE 759 of 792
============================================================
Glossary of Interview Supervision Terms 731 
tions convey a sense of careful listening and concern. They fre¬ 
quently begin with phrases such as, "Earlier you had mentioned 
. . ." or "At one point you had talked about . . . 
Region 
A region is a facilic analysis term referring to an area of inter¬ 
view in which a specific topic is being explored or in which the 
clinician is utilizing a specific interviewing tool focused on the 
process as opposed to the content of the interview (see Content 
Region and Process Regions). 
Rehearsed Interview 
This interview is characterized by a moderate to large produc¬ 
tion of spontaneous speech that focuses only on topics of interest to 
the patient. It is a method by which the interview is controlled by 
the patient. 
Responsive Zone 
The range of distances between the clinician and the patient in 
which both participants feel comfortable and seem to be aware, at 
least at an unconscious level, of each other's movements. Once 
within the responsive zone (RZ), nonverbal behaviors of the clinician 
have the potential to effectively impact upon the patient. Inside the 
RZ, nonverbal behaviors may be intimidating or overwhelming. 
Outside the RZ, nonverbal behaviors may seem insignificant and 
noneffective. 
Scouting Period 
Generally the first 5 to 7 minutes of a 50- to 60-minute interview. 
This period is composed of both the introduction and the opening 
phase of the interview. Open-ended verbalizations tend to predomi¬ 
nate, for the main goal of the clinician is the development of a sound 
engagement with the patient. One or two empathic statements are 
frequently useful. 
Shame Attenuation 
A technique for increasing the validity of the patient's response 
by maintaining a nonthreatening stance. The question tends to be 

============================================================
PAGE 760 of 792
============================================================
732 Glossary of Interview Supervision Terms 
phrased in such a fashion that a positive reply does not feel incrimi¬ 
nating to the patient but suggests a specific problem to the clinician. 
A classic example would be the question, “Do you find that other 
men pick fights with you when you're trying to enjoy a drink at the 
bar?" If the patient answers yes, it is easy to discover the number 
of fights and whether the patient actually plays a role in their 
initiation, as seen in sociopathy. 
Shut-Down Interview 
In this type of interview, the patient answers tersely and shows 
little spontaneity or interest in engagement. Eye contact is frequently 
poor, and gesturing is at a minimum. A variant of this style is the 
hostile interview, in which the patient's tone of voice may be angry 
and the content of the patient's responses may be sharp or antago¬ 
nistic. In the hostile interview, eye contact may be prolonged and 
glaring. 
Side-Tracking 
Side-tracking is a method of handling resistance in which the 
clinician switches topics away from the issue raised by the patient. 
It can be very effective with tangential patients, especially if the 
clinician chooses a topic about which the patient is invested in 
talking. If the patient returns to the original resistance, it suggests 
that side-tracking will not work. 
Spontaneous Gate 
A smooth transition from one topic to another that is initiated 
by the patient. The clinician completes the transition by asking 
questions of relevance to the new topic. 
Statement of Inquiry 
These statements follow the declarative pattern of subject/verb. 
They represent statements, but the tone of voice suggests*that they 
are intended to be inquiries and are followed by a question mark. 
Two examples are as follows: (1) "You were always late for work?" 
and (2) "So you were hesitant to go to college?" In shut-down 
interviews these statements of inquiry can be answered tersely. On 
a positive side, they may function to effectively clarify questions or 

============================================================
PAGE 761 of 792
============================================================
Glossary of Interview Supervision Terms 733 
to summarize the patient's story. On a negative side, they are leading 
questions. 
Stilted Expansion 
An exploration of a given topic in which the clinician's ques¬ 
tioning seems highly structured and awkward. It is sometimes seen 
when clinicians utilize a checklist approach to symptoms or when 
the transitions between questions are repeatedly abrupt or odd. 
Stilted expansions can create a "Meet the Press" style of interaction. 
Swing Questions 
Questions that essentially ask the interviewee whether he or 
she would like to answer or not. They begin with phrases such as, 
"Could you tell me . . .", "Would you say something about . . .", 
or "Can you describe . . ." If engagement is high, such swing 
questions function essentially as open-ended questions. But if en¬ 
gagement is low, they can easily be answered tersely and hence tend 
to further shut down the interviewee, functioning as closed-ended 
inquires. 
Symptom Amplification 
A technique for increasing the validity of a patient's response 
by setting the upper limits of a question at such a high level that 
when the patient downplays the amount, the remaining sum still 
suggests a problem. For example, "How much liquor can you hold 
at one time, say a pint or a fifth?" If the patient responds "Oh no, 
not that much, maybe a pint, perhaps a pint and a half at most" 
then the clinician is still aware that a significantly high tolerance is 
present. 
Tracking 
The ability of the clinician to sensitively follow the patient's 
train of thoughts with appropriate clarifying questions. The clinician 
can track both the affect of the patient or the content of the 
patient's speech. 
Unipolar Blending 
A specific type of blending that occurs when the patient appears 
immediately and unusually at ease with the clinician. This "high 

============================================================
PAGE 762 of 792
============================================================
734 Glossary of Interview Supervision Terms 
engagement" is in reality relatively superficial and arises essentially 
independently of the clinician's engagement techniques. The patient 
is often talkative and may be dramatic. The presence of unipolar 
blending frequently suggests underlying psychopathology such as 
histrionic traits, a hypomanic or manic state, or acute intoxication. 
Type A Validity Errors 
These errors are related to the content of the interviewer's 
questions and include leading questions, negative questions, multi¬ 
ple questions, and other phrasing errors. 
Type B Validity Errors 
These errors are related to paralanguage and nonverbal activity. 
They include processes such as a curt tone of voice, a horizontal 
head nod while asking a question, or inappropriately cutting pa¬ 
tients off. 
Wandering Interview 
An interview characterized by a patient with verbose speech 
production and tangential or circumstantial thought. A variant of 
this interview type is the loquacious interview, in which the patient 
produces large amounts of speech but the speech stays on the chosen 
topic. The wandering interview is furthered by poor structuring 
techniques on the part of the clinician.
